did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to classify dermoscopic images into malignant and benign melanoma. The method involved segmenting images using an active contour model and extracting texture and color features, achieving 97% accuracy on the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a new algorithm for melanoma detection. It details specific methods including image segmentation using an active contour model, extraction of color and texture features (VMS and turn count), and classification using a Support Vector Machine (SVM). The study reports original results, achieving 97% accuracy on the ISIC dataset, which distinguishes it from a review paper.

title: Melanoma skin cancer detection using color and new texture features
brief_summary: This study presents a new algorithm to classify dermoscopic images into malignant and benign melanoma. The method involves segmenting images using an active contour model and extracting color and new texture features, specifically turn count (TC) from wavelet approximation matrices. The extracted features were classified using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier. The system was evaluated on the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, which contained 60 images of benign and malignant melanomas. The proposed approach achieved an accuracy of 97%, demonstrating high efficacy in diagnosing melanoma compared to existing methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies dermoscopic images into malignant and benign melanoma. The dataset used (ISIC_UDA-2_1) contained 23 images of benign melanomas and 37 images of malignant melanomas.
Mentioned in the context of a related work by Hamd and Asa who classified skin lesions into melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma. Not used in the current study's experiments.
Mentioned in the context of a related work by Hamd and Asa who classified skin lesions into melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma. Not used in the current study's experiments.
Mentioned in the introduction as a benign skin lesion similar to melanoma, cited from reference [4]. Not used in the current study's experiments.

all_task_types.details: The paper classifies dermoscopic images into two discrete diagnostic categories: malignant melanoma and benign melanoma. The study uses a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier on extracted color and texture features to predict these binary classes, achieving 97% accuracy on the ISIC dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC_UDA-2_1
datasets_used.description: A subset of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset containing dermoscopy images of melanoma lesions. The dataset includes manual bounding boxes placed around the lesions.

use.training: Used for training the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for validation via 10-fold cross-validation technique.
use.testing: Used for evaluation/testing of the proposed method.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for validating the training phase of the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity metrics using 10-fold cross-validation.

ISIC_UDA-2_1.description: Dataset containing 60 images (23 benign, 37 malignant melanomas). Results evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation.
ISIC_UDA-2_1.notes: The proposed method combining texture (VMS and TC) and color features achieved the highest performance. Table 4 in the paper compares this result (97% accuracy) with existing methods such as Codella et al. (93.10%), Sheha et al. (92%), Antony (86.66%), Immagulate and Vijaya (93%), Thirumavalavan and Jayaraman (86.36%), and Kasmi and Mokrani (94%).

architecture_used.input_size: 150 x 150 pixels (fixed dimensions of the segmented optimal district)

architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: Manual feature extraction using color components (R, G, B) and new texture features (Variance Mean Square (VMS) and Turn Count (TC)) derived from 2D discrete wavelet transform (3-level decomposition) approximation matrices.
architecture_details.segmentation_method: Active contour model (Mumford-Shah segmentation and level set methods)

hyperparameters.turn_count_threshold: 0.5 * standard deviation of the analyzed signal

preprocessing_techniques: Segmentation using active contour model (Mumford-Shah segmentation and level set methods) to isolate the optimal district of the lesion.
User-defined initial optimal district placement to assist segmentation.
Fixing dimensions of the segmented optimal district to 150 x 150 pixels.
Separation of R, G, and B color components from the Region of Interest (ROI).
Removal of zero values from the color component matrices.
Normalization of the color component matrices.
conclusions: The proposed method, which combines color features (RGB components) and new texture features (Variance Mean Square and Turn Count) extracted via wavelet transform and classified by a Support Vector Machine (SVM), achieved an accuracy of 97% on the ISIC dataset.
Texture-based features, specifically Turn Count (TC) derived from wavelet approximation matrices, demonstrated high efficacy, with TC alone achieving 91.7% accuracy and 100% sensitivity.

The combination of all extracted features (color and texture) yielded the best performance (97% accuracy, 97% specificity, 96% sensitivity) compared to using color features alone (78% accuracy) or texture features alone (93% accuracy).

The proposed system outperformed several existing methods cited in the study, including those by Codella et al. (93.10%), Sheha et al. (92%), Antony (86.66%), Immagulate and Vijaya (93%), Thirumavalavan and Jayaraman (86.36%), and Kasmi and Mokrani (94%).

The study acknowledges that many studies on large and complete databases are needed to increase the performance and speed of diagnosis, indicating a limitation regarding the small dataset size (60 images) used in this work.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a very small dataset size (60 images: 23 benign, 37 malignant), which the authors acknowledge requires expansion to increase performance and generalizability.

The dataset (ISIC_UDA-2_1) contains images from patients aged 10-90 years with at least 16 women, but specific demographic distribution details are not fully reported, raising potential population bias concerns.
The segmentation process relies on user-defined initial optimal district placement, introducing potential subjectivity and inter-operator variability in the preprocessing step.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the reported accuracy (97%), specificity (97%), and sensitivity (96%).

The comparison with existing methods in Table 4 is heterogeneous, citing studies with different lesion types (e.g., melanocytic nevi, non-melanoma skin cancer) and varying class definitions, making direct performance comparison difficult.
The study was published in a conference proceeding (AISP 2017) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may impact the rigor of the external validation and peer review process.
No external validation on a separate, independent dataset was performed; evaluation was conducted solely via 10-fold cross-validation on the single ISIC subset.
